title: Wire Claude chat panel to Tauri sidecar IPC
analysis:
The Claude chat panel UI is fully implemented (claudeStore with state management, ChatPanel component, OutboundMessage types) and the Tauri backend exposes claude_send_message, claude_abort, claude_clear_session commands plus 7 claude-* event types. The gap is the bridge layer: bridge.ts has no Claude IPC functions, App.tsx passes no-op callbacks to createClaudeStore, and there's no subscription to claude-* Tauri events.

Existing patterns are clear and consistent:
- bridge.ts wraps invoke() calls as typed async functions (lines 21-108) and listen() calls as typed event subscribers returning UnlistenFn (lines 110-191)
- App.tsx uses an alive flag + try/catch pattern for event subscription with defensive cleanup
- engineStore.subscribeToEvents() uses Promise.allSettled to subscribe to multiple events and returns a combined unlisten function
- bridge.test.ts mocks @tauri-apps/api/core invoke and @tauri-apps/api/event listen using vi.mock(), tests invoke args and event payload extraction

The OutboundMessage type lives at gui/sidecar/src/types.ts with 7 variants: text_delta, thinking_delta, tool_call, tool_result, done, error, ready. The store's MessageContext uses camelCase (selectedEntity) while Rust backend expects snake_case (selected_entity) - the bridge must handle this translation.

The implementation adds: (1) 3 invoke wrappers for claude commands, (2) a subscribeToClaudeEvents function that subscribes to all 7 event types and maps payloads to OutboundMessage, (3) App.tsx wiring of callbacks and event subscription with proper cleanup.

Post-review update: subscribeToClaudeEvents had a resource leak — Promise.all over 7 listen() calls could orphan already-registered listeners if a later call fails. Steps 8-9 fix this with a sequential try-catch-with-rollback pattern.

Post-review update 2: Steps 10-11 add missing payload-mapping tests for claude-thinking-delta and claude-tool-result. ThinkingDelta has { id, content } (same shape as TextDelta — easy to mask a bug) and ToolResult has { id, tool_name, result } (distinct from ToolCall's tool_input). The spread pattern { type: 'tool_result', ...event.payload } was untested, so a wrong field name or missing field would go undetected.

steps.description: Write failing tests for claudeSendMessage, claudeAbort, claudeClearSession invoke wrappers in gui/src/__tests__/claudeBridge.test.ts. Tests import the functions from bridge.ts (which don't exist yet), mock @tauri-apps/api/core invoke, and assert each calls invoke() with the correct command name and arguments. claudeSendMessage must map camelCase context fields to snake_case. Follow existing bridge.test.ts patterns: vi.mock before imports, vi.mocked(invoke), beforeEach vi.clearAllMocks.

Add claudeSendMessage, claudeAbort, claudeClearSession invoke wrappers to gui/src/bridge.ts after line 108 (after lspRequest, before event listeners section). claudeSendMessage accepts (text: string, context?: { selectedEntity?, diagnostics?, constraints? }) and maps to invoke('claude_send_message', { text, context: { selected_entity, diagnostics, constraints } }). claudeAbort calls invoke('claude_abort'). claudeClearSession calls invoke('claude_clear_session'). All return typed Promises.

Add failing tests for subscribeToClaudeEvents in claudeBridge.test.ts. Test that it calls listen() for all 7 event names: claude-text-delta, claude-thinking-delta, claude-tool-call, claude-tool-result, claude-done, claude-error, claude-ready. Test that the combined unlisten function calls all individual unlisteners. Test that firing a mock claude-text-delta event invokes the handler with { type: 'text_delta', id, content }. Test claude-tool-call maps payload to { type: 'tool_call', id, tool_name, tool_input }. Test claude-ready maps to { type: 'ready' }.

Add subscribeToClaudeEvents(handler: (msg: OutboundMessage) => void): Promise<() => void> to gui/src/bridge.ts after the invoke wrappers. Import OutboundMessage from '../../sidecar/src/types'. Use Promise.all to listen to all 7 claude-* events, mapping each event payload to the correct OutboundMessage variant. Return a combined unlisten function that calls all individual unlisteners. Follow the pattern from engineStore.subscribeToEvents but keep it in bridge.ts since it's a bridge-layer concern.

Write failing integration test in gui/src/__tests__/claudeIntegration.test.ts. Mock Tauri invoke/listen. Create a claudeStore with bridge-wired callbacks (onSend calls claudeSendMessage, onAbort calls claudeAbort). Test: calling store.sendMessage triggers invoke('claude_send_message', ...) with correct args. Simulate claude-text-delta and claude-done events via captured listen handlers. Assert store.state.messages contains the assistant message with correct responseText and complete=true.

Wire App.tsx: (1) Add imports for claudeSendMessage, claudeAbort, subscribeToClaudeEvents from './bridge'. (2) Replace no-op callbacks at lines 60-63 with real implementations: onSend calls claudeSendMessage(text, context) with camelCase-to-object mapping, .catch adds system message via claudeStore.addSystemMessage; onAbort calls claudeAbort().catch(console.error). (3) Add claudeEventUnsub variable alongside unsub/fileChangedUnsub. (4) In initApp after engine event subscription (~line 221), add subscribeToClaudeEvents(claudeStore.handleOutboundMessage) with alive guard and error toast. (5) Add claudeEventUnsub?.() to onCleanup and to the defensive cleanup at top of initApp.

Run all existing tests (vitest) to verify no regressions. The claudeBridge and integration tests should pass. Existing bridge.test.ts, claudeStore.test.ts, App.test.tsx, ChatPanel.test.tsx should all still pass. Fix any import or type errors that surface.

Add failing test for listener rollback on partial failure in gui/src/__tests__/claudeBridge.test.ts. Mock listen() to succeed for the first 3 calls (returning unlisten fns), then reject on the 4th call. Assert that: (1) subscribeToClaudeEvents rejects with the error from the 4th listen(), (2) all 3 previously-resolved unlisten functions were called (cleaning up orphaned listeners), (3) no listeners are leaked. Add a second test: listen() fails on the very first call — assert subscribeToClaudeEvents rejects and no unlisteners are called (none were registered). Add a third test: listen() fails on the last (7th) call — assert all 6 prior unlisteners are called before the rejection propagates.

Replace Promise.all in subscribeToClaudeEvents (bridge.ts lines 153-181) with a sequential try-catch-with-rollback pattern. Define the event-name/mapper entries as an array of tuples. Loop sequentially: const unlisteners: UnlistenFn[] = []; try { for (const [name, mapper] of entries) { unlisteners.push(await listen(name, mapper)); } } catch (err) { unlisteners.forEach(u => u()); throw err; } Return the combined unlisten function as before. This ensures that if any listen() call fails, all already-registered listeners are torn down before the error propagates to the caller.

Add two missing payload-mapping tests to gui/src/__tests__/claudeBridge.test.ts in the subscribeToClaudeEvents describe block. (1) 'maps claude-thinking-delta event to OutboundMessage { type: "thinking_delta" }': mock listen() to capture the handler for 'claude-thinking-delta', fire it with payload { id: 'msg-t1', content: 'Let me think...' }, assert handler received { type: 'thinking_delta', id: 'msg-t1', content: 'Let me think...' }. Follow the exact same capturedHandler pattern used by the existing claude-text-delta test. (2) 'maps claude-tool-result event to OutboundMessage { type: "tool_result" }': mock listen() to capture the handler for 'claude-tool-result', fire it with payload { id: 'msg-tr1', tool_name: 'read_file', result: 'file contents here' }, assert handler received { type: 'tool_result', id: 'msg-tr1', tool_name: 'read_file', result: 'file contents here' }. This specifically tests the distinct ToolResult shape (result: unknown) vs ToolCall's tool_input field.

Run the full vitest suite to verify the two new payload-mapping tests from step-10 pass against the existing subscribeToClaudeEvents implementation in bridge.ts. The spread pattern { type: 'tool_result', ...event.payload } should correctly map the ToolResult fields. If any test fails (e.g., field name mismatch or missing field), fix the corresponding entry in the entries array in subscribeToClaudeEvents. All existing tests must continue passing — no regressions.

design_decisions.decision: Put subscribeToClaudeEvents in bridge.ts rather than a separate claude bridge module
design_decisions.rationale: The existing engine event listeners (onMeshUpdate etc.) are already in bridge.ts. A single subscribeToClaudeEvents function that combines all 7 listeners is analogous to how engineStore.subscribeToEvents works but kept in the bridge layer where all Tauri IPC lives. This avoids an unnecessary new file while maintaining the established pattern.

design_decisions.decision: Use sequential listen() with try-catch rollback instead of Promise.all for subscribeToClaudeEvents
design_decisions.rationale: Promise.all over 7 listen() calls creates a resource leak: if the Nth call rejects, the N-1 already-resolved unlisteners are never captured and cannot be cleaned up. The sequential pattern collects unlisteners as they resolve; on any failure it calls all collected unlisteners before re-throwing. This guarantees no orphaned Tauri event listeners regardless of which call fails. Originally Promise.all was chosen for conciseness, but correctness under failure trumps brevity.

design_decisions.decision: Bridge layer handles camelCase-to-snake_case context mapping
design_decisions.rationale: The store uses camelCase (selectedEntity) matching TypeScript conventions, while Rust uses snake_case (selected_entity). The bridge is the natural translation boundary between TS and Rust worlds, just as it handles RawMeshData conversion for engine events.

design_decisions.decision: onSend callback uses fire-and-forget with .catch error handling
design_decisions.rationale: The store's onSend callback is synchronous (returns void), so we can't await the invoke. Instead, claudeSendMessage is called and .catch adds a system error message to the store. This matches the pattern used for other bridge calls in App.tsx (e.g., bridgeSetParameter at line 257).

design_decisions.decision: Import OutboundMessage from sidecar types in bridge.ts
design_decisions.rationale: The OutboundMessage type at gui/sidecar/src/types.ts is the canonical wire protocol type. The bridge layer maps Tauri event payloads to this type, and the claudeStore already imports it via the same path (../../sidecar/src/types from stores/). From bridge.ts the path is ../sidecar/src/types.

design_decisions.decision: Test every event type's payload mapping individually even when shapes overlap
design_decisions.rationale: ThinkingDelta and TextDelta share the same { id, content } shape, and ToolResult and ToolCall differ only in result vs tool_input. The spread pattern { type: X, ...event.payload } could silently pass through wrong fields or miss renames. Testing each variant individually catches field name mismatches that structural similarity would otherwise mask.

reuse.how: Copy the vi.mock('@tauri-apps/api/core') and vi.mock('@tauri-apps/api/event') pattern, vi.mocked() accessors, and mockImplementation for simulating event delivery. Reuse beforeEach vi.clearAllMocks.

reuse.what: Event subscription with alive guard pattern
reuse.how: Replicate the try { const u = await subscribe(); if (!alive) { u(); return; } unsub = u; } catch pattern for Claude event subscription.
Use the same .catch(err => showToast/addSystemMessage) pattern for claudeSendMessage and claudeAbort error handling.
Import OutboundMessage type in bridge.ts for the subscribeToClaudeEvents handler parameter and for constructing typed event payloads.
The integration test creates a claudeStore with overridable callbacks, similar to how claudeStore.test.ts creates stores for testing state transitions.
Add claudeEventUnsub cleanup to the same defensive cleanup block at the top of initApp and in onCleanup.

The new thinking_delta and tool_result tests reuse the exact same mockListen.mockImplementation pattern that captures the handler for a specific event name, then fires it with a test payload and asserts the handler received the mapped OutboundMessage.
